title: Retrieval Processing
html:
Retrieval Processing

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication
Watch
Download PDF
Save selected topicSave selected topic and subtopicsSave entire publication
Share
Email LinkCopy Link
Feedback
  • Last UpdatedAug 22, 2024
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

Data is retrieved from the database when the user enters a retrieve profile (that is, a search string) in the desired fields and then enters ^RETRIEVE. Alternatively, ProcScript can be used to assign retrieve profiles to the desired fields in a component. When retrieving data from the database, Uniface selects the required data with the Select connector request, and fetches it into the component with the Fetch connector request.

The basic process is as follows:

  1. A ^RETRIEVE instruction with retrieve profile is issued by the user or from ProcScript, which in turn activates the appropriate triggers and ProcScript instructions. For more information, see Uniface Database I/O.
  2. Uniface makes a Select call to the DBMS connector. When called with Select, the DBMS connector tries to select the data from the database, using the retrieve profile as the argument.
  3. The results that match the retrieve profile are returned in a hitlist or select cache. The result of a Select request varies between Uniface versions and operating systems. If a record is approximately half a kilobyte or smaller, Uniface uses the select cache mechanism. If larger, the hitlist mechanism is used.
  4. Uniface makes a Fetch call to bring occurrences into the hitlist or select cache. Uniface always fetches the occurrences that are visible (that is, the data which fills the painted occurrences on the component). Structure editor functions such as ^NEXT_OCC also cause a Fetch, if the next occurrence is not visible and has not been made.

    Fetch always pulls a complete record into Uniface. To improve performance, the field list for an entity can cause some of the fields to not be formatted within Uniface. If fields are not in the component structure, or referred to in ProcScript modules, they are not available to the user. Therefore, if an occurrence exists in the hitlist, complete primary key information should be available.

Matching the Retrieve Profile

A retrieve profile is a search string. It is a range of one or more values for one or more fields in an entity, often containing profile characters, that can be used to control the scope of the data to be retrieved from the database. With the exception of system errors, the retrieve profile can only match one of the following:

  • Nothing. If no occurrence is found, the retrieve process stops and no more calls are made to the DBMS connector in the current attempt to retrieve data.
  • One occurrence. If one occurrence is found, Uniface makes the Fetch request to the DBMS connector and fetches that occurrence into the component.
  • More than one occurrence. If more than one occurrence in the database matches the retrieve profile, what happens next depends on the following factors:
    • The number of occurrences of that entity painted on the component.
    • The size of each occurrence.

In SQL-based DBMSs, retrieve profiles can contain characters which SQL-based DBMSs treat as profile characters but which Uniface does not. For example, the underscore (_) is a wildcard for Oracle and Informix. When the connector generates the resulting select statement, it has to treat such characters as wildcards if using the like operator. For example, a profile of c_c retrieves c_c, but c_c* retrieves both c_c and ccc.

Hitlist

Uniface builds a temporary list of the occurrences which are not visible, but which have been found in the database and selected with the Select connector request. This is referred to as the hitlist because each occurrence that matches the retrieve profile is a successful hit on the database.

The Select request selects the primary key or physical record address of an occurrence and places it into the hitlist. The Fetch request uses this information to retrieve the complete record from the database into the component.

When possible, Uniface saves on database I/O by not building a hitlist of all the matching occurrences. Instead, the Select request is used multiple times to pass matching occurrences in a number of steps. Either the DBMS or the connector maintains a status to determine which occurrences must still be added to the hitlist. A partially built hitlist is called a stepped hitlist.

Note: Not all DBMSs support stepped hitlists, and those that do support them cannot use the default value of ten occurrences in a step.

Select Cache

If an occurrence contains less than a certain amount of data (as determined by the $MAX_CACHE_SIZE assignment setting), the whole occurrence is retrieved into the hitlist (rather than just the primary key or physical record address). This is referred to as the select cache. The select cache usually improves performance because the Fetch request fetches the record from the cache instead of the database. This saves on database I/O.

If the maximum cache size is not specified, Uniface uses the default value of approximately half a kilobyte, varying between Uniface versions and operating systems. In any case, the list either contains the actual occurrences themselves, or the means for fetching them quickly.

If the Select has already built a hitlist of primary key values or physical record addresses, the hitlist could be described as being an invisible list of exact retrieve profiles for all occurrences required by the user or component at any particular time. The actual fetch on this data is fast.

To have each occurrence retrieved into the select cache when moving to the last occurrence, you can set the $ENABLE_CACHE_ALWAYS assignment setting.

Specifying an Index

For record-level DBMSs, the read statement's using clause allows you to specify which index Uniface should use. The index argument is a numeric expression that evaluates to the index number. For more information on the availability of the using clause, see the DBMS connector documentation for your DBMS.

Related Links
  • Data Retrieval Support

    In response to read and selectdb ProcScript instructions, Uniface issues connector requests to select data into a hitlist and fetch it into the calling component. Special considerations and restrictions may apply for some databases.

  • Retrieve Profile Characters

    A retrieve profile is used to control the scope of the data to be retrieved from the database. It consists of one or more values for one or more fields in an entity, often containing profile characters. In essence, a retrieve profile is a search query.

  • Stepped Hitlist and Select Cache

    When retrieving data from a database, Uniface stores the retrieved records into an internal Uniface data structure called the stepped hitlist. This mechanism retrieves groups of records into the component, instead retrieving the complete table, which can cause the user to experience considerable delays.

  • $ENABLE_CACHE_ALWAYS

    Allow the select cache mechanism when moving to the last occurrence.

  • $MAX_CACHE_SIZE

    Set the maximum size of occurrence that is retrieved in the select cache after subsetting.

  • Database Connectors

    To access, retrieve, and store data, Uniface provides database connectors that translate Uniface I/O requests into the target database's data definition and manipulation languages.
